text: A transverse incision is made in the peritoneum, extending from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical ligament, exposing the underlying structures for the hernia repair.
The surgeon makes a transverse incision in the peritoneum, starting from the anterior superior iliac spine and extending towards the medial umbilical ligament.
The surgeon enters the preperitoneal space and creates a preperitoneal flap, preparing the area for further dissection and mesh placement during the robotic-assisted hernia repair.

The surgeon continues the dissection inferiorly, carefully maneuvering around the hernia sac using robotic assistance.

The robotic instruments are used to perform a precise dissection around the hernia sac, carefully freeing it from surrounding adhesions.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to completely reduce the hernia sac and preperitoneal fat that were incarcerated into the hernia defect, visibly freeing these structures from the defect.

The surgeon identifies the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament, visually confirming their anatomical landmarks within the surgical field.

The surgeon continues the dissection laterally, expanding the preperitoneal space and further freeing the hernia sac from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon identifies a small indirect hernia containing preperitoneal fat, which is visibly reduced from the hernia defect.

The surgeon continues the dissection superiorly and inferiorly to clear the attachments of the peritoneal flap over the testicular vessels and vas deferens, using robotic instruments for precision.

The surgeon inserts a large mesh foam bar into the surgical space, positioning it over the terminal defects to reinforce the area.

The surgeon sutures the mesh to Cooper's ligament and the abdominal wall to address the large direct inguinal hernia defect.

The surgeon uses a few interrupted sutures to secure the mesh to the Cooper's ligament and abdominal wall, with the suturing tool positioned at the bottom of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a tool to place a few interrupted sutures at the bottom of the surgical field, securing the mesh in place.
The surgeon secures the remaining portion of the mesh to the abdominal wall using T-seal, ensuring the mesh is firmly anchored in place.

The surgeon closes the peritoneal defect using an absorbable two OB lock suture, applying a simple running stitch in two layers.

The surgeon incorporates the preperitoneal fat, previously reduced from the hernia defect, into the closure of the peritoneum, ensuring it is included within the sutured layers.
